skill.name: codebase-design
skill.description: Shared vocabulary for designing deep modules. Use when the user wants to design or improve a module's interface, find deepening opportunities, decide where a seam goes, make code more testable or AI-navigable, or when another skill needs the deep-module vocabulary.
skill.summary: A methodology and vocabulary skill for designing deep modules with small interfaces and large implementations, placed at clean seams.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: The codebase-design skill is a pure documentation skill providing vocabulary and methodology for designing deep modules. All 35 static findings are false positives: 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' detections are triggered by the word 'deep' (confused with DES cipher references in unrelated codebases), 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' matches markdown backtick formatting for code blocks, and 'System reconnaissance' is a misidentification of architectural vocabulary. No executable code, scripts, or data flows exist in any file.

content.user_title: Design deep modules with clear interfaces
content.value_statement: Most codebases suffer from shallow modules with large interfaces and thin implementations. This skill provides a shared vocabulary and methodology for designing deep modules, deciding where seams go, and making code more testable and maintainable.
actual_capabilities: Provides a precise vocabulary for module, interface, seam, adapter, depth, leverage, and locality
Offers principles for designing interfaces with fewer methods and simpler parameters
Includes a classification system for dependencies (in-process, local-substitutable, remote but owned, true external)
Provides guidance on seam discipline including when to introduce ports and adapters
Suggests testing strategies based on the replace-don't-layer principle
Includes a parallel sub-agent pattern for exploring radically different interface designs
limitations: Does not generate code or implement refactoring automatically
Vocabulary is opinionated and may conflict with existing team terminology
Requires human judgment to apply the deepening methodology to specific codebases
Does not provide tools for static analysis or measurement of module depth

use_cases.title: Designing a new module interface
use_cases.description: A developer wants to design a clean interface for a new feature with maximum leverage for callers and minimum surface area for tests.

use_cases.title: Refactoring shallow modules into deep ones
use_cases.description: An engineering team has accumulated shallow modules with thin implementations and wants to consolidate them into deeper, more testable units.

use_cases.title: Establishing shared architectural vocabulary
use_cases.description: A team needs a consistent language for discussing module boundaries, seams, and adapters across code reviews and design discussions.

prompt_templates.title: Review a module's interface for depth
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the [module name] interface. Is it deep or shallow? Can I reduce the number of methods or simplify the parameters? What complexity could be hidden inside the implementation?

prompt_templates.title: Find deepening opportunities
prompt_templates.prompt: Look at these [N] related shallow modules. Could merging them create a deeper module? Classify their dependencies as in-process, local-substitutable, remote-but-owned, or true external.

prompt_templates.title: Decide where to place a seam
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to decide where the seam should go for [feature]. Apply the deletion test: if I delete this module, does complexity vanish or spread? Should there be a port or just an internal seam?

prompt_templates.title: Explore alternative interfaces in parallel
prompt_templates.prompt: Spawn 3 sub-agents to design radically different interfaces for [module]. Agent 1 should minimize the interface, Agent 2 should maximize flexibility, and Agent 3 should optimize for the most common caller.

output_examples.input: Review the processOrder function. Is it a deep module?
output: It is currently shallow: processOrder(order) creates its own StripeGateway internally.
Deep version: processOrder(order, paymentGateway) accepts the gateway as a dependency.
Callers can substitute test or mock gateways without changing the function.
The interface gets one extra parameter, but the module becomes testable.

output_examples.input: How should I classify a Redis cache dependency?
output: Redis is local-substitutable: ioredis-mock or testcontainers-redis can run in tests.
Test with the stand-in running in the test suite.
No port at the external interface, just an internal seam.

best_practices: Use the exact vocabulary from this skill consistently across your team and documentation
Apply the deletion test to verify a module is earning its keep
Only introduce a port when at least two adapters are justified
Write tests at the module's interface, not past it
Design for two adapters (production and test) before adding more
anti_patterns: Substituting 'component', 'service', 'API', or 'boundary' for the precise terms in this skill
Introducing seams with only one adapter
Testing past the interface by reaching into internal state
Treating depth as a ratio of implementation-lines to interface-lines
Keeping old unit tests on shallow modules after consolidation

faq.question: What is a deep module?
faq.answer: A deep module has a small interface but a large implementation. Callers get more capability per unit of interface they have to learn.

faq.question: What is a seam?
faq.answer: A seam is a place where you can alter behaviour without editing in that place. It is the location at which a module's interface lives.

faq.question: When should I introduce a port?
faq.answer: Only when at least two adapters are justified, typically one for production and one for testing. A single-adapter seam is just indirection.

faq.question: How do I know if a module is shallow?
faq.answer: Apply the deletion test: imagine deleting the module. If complexity vanishes, it was a pass-through. If complexity reappears across many callers, it was earning its keep.

faq.question: What is the difference between an interface and an adapter?
faq.answer: An interface is everything a caller must know to use a module. An adapter is a concrete thing that satisfies an interface at a seam.

faq.question: Does this skill work with Claude Code, Codex, and Claude?
faq.answer: Yes. The vocabulary and methodology are tool-agnostic and can be applied across any AI coding assistant or human developer workflow.
